title: Autonomous oscillations in quantum electromechanics: tensor network treatment
authors: Mahasweta Pandit, Sheikh Parvez Mandal, Mark T. Mitchison, Javier Prior
abstract: Transport-induced self-sustained oscillations in electromechanical systems convert a static electrochemical bias into robust, autonomous oscillatory motion in the absence of any external periodic drive. However, an exact description of such self-oscillations remains challenging in nanoscale electromechanical devices featuring a simultaneously large bosonic Hilbert space, strong interactions, and structured fermionic leads. We formulate a tensor-network framework that combines a binary representation of the vibrational mode with mesoscopic reservoir embeddings that enable controlled access to the self-oscillatory steady states and relevant transport observables without explicit real-time propagation. We demonstrate the emergence of mechanical self-oscillations across a broad set of operating conditions, in which strong electromechanical backaction, nonadiabatic oscillator dynamics, and energy-dependent electronic tunneling processes compete. Furthermore, we observe that for both slow and fast vibrating mechanical modes, suppressed vibrational occupation fluctuations in the self-oscillation window along the electromechanical coupling strength sweep is preceded by a peak in the occupation fluctuations. Collectively, we explore how both intrinsic system properties and environmental parameters govern such autonomous oscillations over a broad range of operating conditions. The generality of our framework will enable the method to be employed straightforwardly to more complicated or experimentally relevant scenarios.
summary: This paper introduces a tensor-network method to simulate autonomous oscillations in quantum electromechanical systems. It overcomes the computational challenges of large bosonic spaces and structured environments to accurately describe the transition to limit-cycle dynamics. The work reveals how electromechanical backaction and environmental parameters govern the stability of self-sustained motion.
main_problem: Developing an exact numerical description for self-sustained oscillations in nanoelectromechanical systems (NEMS) where large bosonic Hilbert spaces, strong electron-phonon coupling, and structured fermionic leads coexist.
main_result: The authors present a unified tensor-network framework that successfully captures the emergence of mechanical limit cycles and identifies that a peak in vibrational fluctuations precedes the onset of self-sustained oscillations.
method: A steady-state tensor-network framework using binary encoding for phonons, mesoscopic reservoir embedding for non-Markovian leads, and a variational DMRG solver in Liouville space.
model_or_system: An Anderson-Holstein impurity model consisting of a single-level quantum dot coupled to a quantized vibrational mode, connected to two fermionic leads and a weak bosonic damping bath.
key_observables: Torotropy (phase-space structure), Husimi Q-function, phonon-number Fano factor, second-order coherence g(2)(0), and particle current.
important_parameters: Electromechanical coupling strength (lambda), adiabaticity parameter (gamma = Gamma/omega_0), lead bandwidth (delta), temperature (T), and phonon damping (Gamma_ph).
main_assumptions: The phonon Hilbert space is truncated to a finite Fock basis, and the fermionic reservoirs are approximated using a sum of Lorentzian spectral densities via auxiliary modes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the NEMS setup; Figure 3 illustrates the binary encoding and reservoir embedding; Figure 4 depicts the Liouville-space vectorization; Figure 10 and 11 show convergence tests and benchmarks against semiclassical results.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the NEMS problem, details the tensor-network construction (binary encoding and reservoir embedding), describes the variational Liouville-space solver, presents results on the emergence of self-oscillations and phase-space dynamics, and concludes with convergence and benchmark analyses.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful, unified numerical tool for studying non-equilibrium steady states in open quantum systems, bridging the gap between adiabatic and non-adiabatic regimes in strongly coupled electron-phonon systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic of a NEMS consisting of a quantum dot, with onsite energy ϵ, coupled to two fermionic reservoirs on the left (L) and right (R) that are driven out of equilibrium by an electrochemical bias µL ≥µR. The imbalance induces electron tunneling through the dot, transferring electrical en- ergy between the reservoirs. The dot additionally couples to a vibrational mode with frequency ω0 (denoted as a solid vi- olet wavy line). The vibrational mode is weakly damped by a bosonic vibrational bath of temperature Tph (depicted here as light wavy lines in the background). Self-sustained oscilla- tions in the phonon mode can be induced by the back-action of electron transport through...

Figure 2. Major parameter regimes explored in this work. The leftmost diagram featuring the adiabaticity parameter, γ = Γ/ω0, depicts how increasing Γ with respect to ω0 takes the model to the nonadiabatic regime. In the other dia- grams, λ denotes the dimensionless electromechanical cou- pling strength, the fermionic lead temperature is denoted by T, and δ is the bandwidth of the fermionic lead. The method- ology presented in this work allows us to uncover the physics at the interface of the denoted regimes in a unified framework.

Figure 3. Binary encoding and mesoscopic leads embedding of the transport-driven NEMS model. (a) Anderson–Holstein-type impurity consisting of a resonant electronic level, a quantum dot d coupled to a vibrational mode ˆb of frequency ω0, two fermionic environments (L and R), and a weakly coupled phonon bath. (b) Binary encoding of the truncated phonon Hilbert space with a cutoff M is represented by N = log2 M hard-core-boson pseudosites ˆaj, as in Eq. (12). (c) Mesoscopic-lead embedding of the structured fermionic reservoirs into damped auxiliary modes governed by Lindblad dissipators, Eq. (20).

Figure 4. Liouville-space vectorization of the density matrix ρ is initially written as an MPO over the mesoscopic lead sites, dot, and phonon pseudosites (top row). Acting on the left vac- uum ∣1⟩⟩(second row) gives the vectorized state ∣ρ⟩⟩= ρ∣1⟩⟩ (third row), represented as an MPS in the doubled space. In the bottom row, pink and green sites denote left and right mesoscopic lead sites, orange denotes the dot, and gray de- notes phonon pseudosites; sites with(-out) boundaries indi- cate the physical (tilde) copies.
Figure 5. Variational steady-state condition depicted in Ten- sor network form. The NESS satisfies L∣ρ⟩⟩= 0 and is ob- tained by variationally minimizing the positive semidefinite form ⟨⟨ρ∣L†L∣ρ⟩⟩.
